conditions.notes: Catalyzed by MnO2, or slow decomposition above 60C
Slow at room temperature; heating accelerates. Iron is moderately reactive.
Haber process: Fe catalyst, 400-500C, 150-300 atm for industrial yield
Requires concentrated nitric acid
Classic chlorine gas preparation. Requires concentrated HCl.
Iodometric titration — iodine color disappears at endpoint
Thiosulfate decomposition — turbidity from sulfur, pungent SO2
Thermochromic — loses water of crystallization, turns from pink to deep blue
Orange dichromate turns green (Cr3+), releases Cl2 gas
White PbCl2 precipitate — dissolves in hot water
Pink/blue precipitate depending on conditions
Very vigorous with dilute H2SO4
Slow at room temperature, produces green solution
Sn2+ reduces Fe3+ to Fe2+, color change from yellow-brown to pale green
Triiodide formation — dissolves iodine into dark brown solution (Lugol's reagent)
Orange dichromate in acid — forms acidified dichromate solution used as oxidizer
Sugar dehydration — concentrated H2SO4 removes water, leaving carbon tower. Highly exothermic.
Urea hydrolysis — endothermic dissolution, slow at room temp, faster with urease enzyme
Halogen displacement — Br2 oxidizes I- to I2, brown bromine color replaced by purple iodine
Bright yellow precipitate of silver iodide — photosensitive
Copper burns in chlorine gas with green flame
Strongly endothermic dissolution — used in cold packs
Strongly exothermic dissolution — used in heat packs
Magnesium burns in CO2 — cannot be extinguished with CO2 fire extinguisher!
Cu2+ reduced to Cu+ by iodide, liberating I2 — brown color appears
Fermentation of glucose by yeast produces ethanol and carbon dioxide
Limewater test for CO2 — solution turns milky white
Gas-phase reaction produces white smoke of ammonium chloride
Laboratory preparation of hydrogen gas
Blood-red color test for Fe3+ ions — thiocyanate complex
Antacid reaction — milk of magnesia neutralizing stomach acid
Double driving force: neutralization AND precipitation of BaSO4
Blue copper hydroxide decomposes to black copper oxide on heating
Iron hydroxide decomposes to iron oxide (rust) on heating
Lead sulfide is extremely insoluble — black precipitate
Chrome yellow — historically used as a pigment
Brick-red precipitate — used as indicator in Mohr titration
Electrolysis of water — requires electrical energy input
White gelatinous precipitate — amphoteric hydroxide, dissolves in excess NaOH
Chlorine displaces bromine — solution turns orange-brown
Bromine displaces iodine — solution turns dark brown/purple
Hot concentrated sulfuric acid is an oxidizing acid — reacts with Cu
Thermite reaction — extremely exothermic, produces molten iron
Reaction slows as insoluble CaSO4 coat forms on marble
Thermal decomposition of magnesium hydroxide
Ammonium bicarbonate decomposes easily — used as leavening agent
Fischer esterification — ethyl acetate has fruity smell
Fischer esterification — methyl acetate
Aluminum dissolves in sodium hydroxide — amphoteric metal
Iron reacts with steam at high temperature to form magnetite
Zinc dissolves in sodium hydroxide — amphoteric metal
Reduction of copper oxide by hydrogen — black turns to copper colored
Blast furnace reduction of iron ore by carbon monoxide
Smelting of iron ore with carbon (coke)
Reduction of copper oxide by carbon
ZnO is amphoteric — dissolves in both acids and bases
Lead chloride precipitates — white needle-like crystals in cold water
Pale yellow precipitate — light sensitive, used in photography
Iron burns in chlorine gas with brown fumes
Magnesium burns in nitrogen — yellow-green powder
Sodium burns in excess oxygen forming sodium peroxide
Quicklime absorbs CO2 to form limestone
Electrophilic aromatic substitution — benzene + bromine with Lewis acid catalyst
Industrial synthesis of ethanol by hydration of ethylene
Catalytic hydrogenation of ethylene to ethane
Lindlar's catalyst for partial hydrogenation of acetylene to ethylene
Calcium carbide + water produces acetylene gas — used in old mining lamps
Baking soda volcano reaction — vinegar + baking soda
Permanganate titration — purple KMnO4 decolorizes as it reacts with Fe2+
Potassium reacts violently with water — lilac flame, may explode
Lithium reacts gently with water — red flame
Calcium reacts with water — solution turns milky as Ca(OH)2 is slightly soluble
Weak acid dissolves washing soda with effervescence
Green gelatinous precipitate of iron(II) hydroxide — darkens in air as it oxidizes to Fe(OH)3
Laboratory preparation of chlorine gas
Sulfite + acid releases sulfur dioxide gas — pungent smell
Rotten egg smell — hydrogen sulfide gas released
Rust-brown gelatinous precipitate of iron(III) hydroxide
Dilute nitric acid dissolves copper — blue solution, colorless NO gas turns brown in air
Potassium nitrate decomposes to nitrite and oxygen on strong heating
Lead nitrate crackles and produces brown NO2 fumes on heating
Copper nitrate decomposes to black CuO and brown NO2 gas
White precipitate of calcium carbonate — water softening reaction
Black precipitate of silver sulfide — similar to tarnishing of silver
Bright yellow precipitate of cadmium sulfide — used as pigment
Cobalt dissolves in HCl forming pink solution
Amphoteric Al(OH)3 dissolves in excess NaOH forming aluminate
